nodes.title: Full repository and specification analysis of Vale
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"first-analysis-with-deciduous","confidence":95,"prompt":"go through the entire repository and its specification and build a full analysis of it using deciduous to note its full layout. Be detail oriented and thorough, it is fine if it takes time. Offer observation nodes that are your own ideas on what is proposed (labelled as AI generated)"}

nodes.title: Map repository structure and infrastructure
Repo has 2 commits: initial spec draft + deciduous bootstrap
Poetry project: Python 3.11+, mkdocs + mkdocs-material for spec rendering
MkDocs Material theme with mermaid diagrams, code highlighting, tabbed content, emoji, footnotes, snippets support
CI/CD: deploy-pages.yml pushes docs/ to gh-pages on main push; cleanup-decision-graphs.yml removes PNG/DOT artifacts after PR merge
docs/ contains single-file decision graph viewer (666KB bundled React app) + empty graph-data.json
.gitignore only excludes Emacs temp files and vale-spec/site (mkdocs build output)
Spec is a single monolithic Markdown file: vale-spec/docs/index.md (~592 lines)
Spec includes two image assets: temper-logo-256.png and temper-logo-mode-dependent.svg for branding
Analyze spec entities and type system
Entity hierarchy: Person (has ID + email + crypto keys), User (sealed: AnyUser | SomeUser), Group (public | p-{name} | g-{number}), Library, Version, Testimonial
Group system: 3 types - 'public' (special singleton), 'p-{name}' (implicit per-person, immutable), 'g-{number}' (named groups, editable). Roles: organizer, member. No negative permissions by design.
User is a sealed interface: AnyUser (anonymous, public-only) vs SomeUser (logged-in, contains Person). User \!= Person by design -- prevents privilege assumption from having a Person reference.
Guarded<T> type: wraps a value with (auto-ID, T value, GroupId visibility gate). Server enforces visibility before domain objects escape to application code.
Ken<T> type: sealed as Known<T> | Unknown<T>. Distinct from nullable -- Unknown means 'not authorized to see', not 'value is absent'. Prevents ambiguity.
Pii<T> type: wraps PII fields. toString() returns 'PII' to prevent log leakage. privilegedGetPii() is greppable for auditing. JSON encode/decode is transparent but must repack on decode.

Delta types: for each domain type T there is a TDelta. Null field = no change. Enables partial updates, batch operations, wire-efficient forms. Fields never use Ken<T> -- extract T first, then add nullable.
DisplayPreferences: just locale. Intentionally minimal -- more prefs would break /pub equivalence for CDN caching.
AccountInfo schema: id (Int64), email (Pii<String>), providerIdentifier (OAuth provider), subclaim (provider-unique ID). No passwords stored. Email uniqueness enforced transactionally.

PrincipalProfile (sealed): shared by PersonProfile and GroupProfile. Fields: id (Int64), name (Guarded<Pii<String>>), bio (Pii<Guarded<Pii<String>>>), urls (List<Guarded<Pii<String>>>), avatars (List<Guarded<Pii<String>>>), favouriteGroups (List<Guarded<GroupId>>)

LibraryProfile schema: id, name (unique, plain text), versions (List<SemVer>), latestVersionProfile (computed, not stored), tags (TagSet), resources (URLs by classification), created (DateTime). managedBy is in separate LibraryManagement table.
LibraryManagement: id, managedBy (GroupId), visibleTo (GroupId). Separate table enables efficient group->library lookups. Orphaned libraries possible if managing group deleted.
LibraryAutomation: id, secret (bytes), description (text), created (DateTime). One-to-many. Secrets write-only -- web UI can store but never retrieve. Publication system is only reader.
VersionProfile: version (SemVer), docs (Map<Locale, String> as Markdown), published (DateTime). Access gated by parent library visibility.
Testimonial: id, author (PersonId), library (LibraryName), opinion (Markdown), authored (DateTime), visibleTo (GroupId). Intentionally no star rating -- favors nuanced text over aggregated scores.
TestimonialBundle: testimonials (List), page (Int32, zero-indexed), pages (Int32 count). Simple paging wrapper for moderation and listing views.

GroupMembership: gOrganizers/gMembers (List<Int64>), pOrganizers/pMembers (Set<PersonId>). Separates p- from g- members so p- members don't need transitive traversal. Cycles are legal between named groups.
Analyze URL architecture and caching strategy
CDN-first design: /pub/[locale] prefix serves as-if-not-logged-in. Server redirects to /pub when user's view equals public view. No negative permissions guarantees this is always safe.
/json prefix on any URL returns application/json instead of HTML. Can combine with /pub: /pub/[locale]/json/... for cacheable API responses.
Page assets: bundled JS + CSS (cached), locale-specific strings, on-demand CSRF token via import(), media as needed. Single JS, single CSS goal.

URL routes: / (homepage), /p/[personid] (person), /p/[personid]/ts (testimonials), /l/[libraryname] (library), /l/[libraryname]/b/[backendid] (backend-specific), /l/[libraryname]/vs (versions list), /l/[libraryname]/v/[semver] (version detail), /l/[libraryname]/mod (moderation), /g/[groupid] (group), /g/[groupid]/ls (group libraries), /g/[groupid]/edit (group edit)
Person page privacy: /p/[personid] using email form must return 404 if email is not public. Prevents email enumeration.
Client-side rendering model: load JSON -> domain objects -> render HTML client-side. Same logic runs on client and server (Temper's cross-platform value proposition).
CSRF: JS needs session secret via importable module. Cannot embed in cached content. Separate secrets-for-current-session module loaded on demand.
Analyze access control and security model
User/Person firewall: all DB transactions mediated by User object. Response handlers never get unauthorized data. Having a Person reference does NOT grant that person's privileges.

UserContext spawned from User: retrieves domain objects from storage, applies deltas with user's privileges. Tracks boolean 'did any retrieval rely on user privileges?' to answer /pub-equivalence question.

Testing strategy: abstract DB connections allow unit tests to seed entire DB contents. Test for sensitive substrings in domain objects (primary) and optionally in rendered HTML after entity/JS/JSON decode (secondary).
No negative permissions invariant: a person logged in NEVER has fewer permissions than when not-logged-in. This enables the CDN /pub redirect optimization.
Markdown security: all third-party Markdown sanitized after HTML conversion. Must check for unbalanced bidi content (bidi override attacks). Short Markdown subset strips non-inline HTML elements.

PII grep-auditable: Pii<T> wrapper + privilegedGetPii() ensures all PII access is searchable in code. PRs can be auto-flagged for new PII unpacking. Not foolproof (reflection, identifier escapes) but practical.
Auto-assigned IDs must NEVER be reused even after entity deletion -- third-party systems may cache significance. Prevents TOCTOU permission issues.
GuardedDelta merge: client delta cannot change auto-assigned ID. Negative IDs used as client-side placeholders before server sync. Server responds with authoritative ID mapping.
Delta merge safety rule (AI-editing guardrail): merge must NEVER produce Known value when source domain object field is Unknown. Spec explicitly restricts AI editors from introducing such verbiage.
LibraryAutomation secrets: web UI has NO read access to secret DB column. Only the publication system can read secrets. DB could implement via separate storage area behind an identifier.

Library visibility: if user not in visibleTo group, return standard 404 (no info leak about library existence). Publication requires user in BOTH managedBy AND visibleTo (prevents info leakage via version conflicts).
URL security: URLs limited to http/https/mailto. Custom mobile URIs deferred. Person page with email ID returns 404 if email not public (prevents enumeration).
Domain objects have typesOfEditsAllowed bitfield (not in Delta type). If an edit button shows because of user privileges, that proves /pub is not equivalent -- cannot redirect.
Analyze workflows

Account creation flow: OAuth only (no passwords). Steps: 1) OAuth attestation -> AccountInfo, 2) Create PersonProfile (name, private-only), 3) Login flow to set session, 4) Quick-import profile from SO/GH/GL/vcard, 5) Redirect to profile edit with privacy warnings.

Login flow: 1) Ensure HTTP session ID, 2) OAuth claim resolution, 3) On failure: offer create/retry, 4) On success: replace session ID for logged-in user, 5) Redirect to originally-requested URL if applicable.

Logout flow: 1) Erase person association from session, 2) Redirect to / (re-infers locale from non-person sources like Accept-Language/geo). Neither login nor logout involve Vale's own access control.

Library creation flow: 1) Choose unique name, 2) Create LibraryProfile with managedBy=visibleTo=user's p-group, 3) Profile edit page (encourage checking managedBy/visibleTo with favourite group dropdowns), 4) Encourage auto-import URLs from git hosting, 5) Pop-up to encourage visiting versions list with publish instructions.

Publication flow: requires login + in managedBy AND visibleTo. Manual: identify library, auth check, pick non-conflicting semver, pick source (git/tarball), retrieve file tree, verify config.temper.md + temper.out + temper.keep, forward to publication system, wait for result, redirect on success. Automated: ValePublish GitHub Action using stored secrets.

Moderation flow: 3 tabs (Queue/Accepted/Rejected), radio buttons per testimonial (no action/accept/reject/back-to-queue depending on tab), bulk Accept All/Reject All on Queue tab. Client caches testimonial statuses, server responds with deltas. Tab switching is client-side from cache. Apply button with unsaved-changes alert.

Testimonial visibility on library page: authors always see their own (with status indicator if queued/rejected). Other authors' queued/rejected testimonials never shown. Managers see discrete 'queue not empty' note.
Analyze database and storage architecture

Storage goals: 1) Simple relational DB impl, 2) Parallel test isolation without spinning up DBs, 3) Pre-application-code access control, 4) Answerable: 'is /pub sufficient for this page?', 5) Change detection for stale-form UX.

Monotonic version stamps (Int64): production uses monotonic system clock, tests use deterministic counter. MSB reserved: client-minted stamps start at Int64 min and increase. Servers never serve stamps with MSB set.

Group membership algorithm: memoized recursive check handling cycles. Key: set memo[pair]=false before recursion (guarantees halting on cycles). Checks p- members directly, recurses into g- members transitively.
AGE (Apache Graph Extension) for Postgres mentioned as efficient transitive closure computation via Cypher queries. Alternative to recursive application-level code for production.
GroupMembership helper on UserContext: caches membership checks so repeated queries are efficient regardless of implementation (DB vs in-memory).

Library documentation extraction: README*.md files in library root, locale mapped via filename (README.[bcp47-tag].md), README.md defaults to 'en'. Snippet priority: og:description > description > title from front-matter > first H1 > library name.
Storage abstraction goal: separate table for LibraryManagement (double-keyed, many:1) enables efficient group->library lookups at /g/[groupid]/ls. latestVersionProfile computed, not stored.
PII storage isolation: storage implementations backed by real DBs must run WITHOUT permissions to billing/marketing databases or tables. No stipulation of real-person correlation.
AI-generated observations on the specification

[AI GENERATED - Strength] The no-negative-permissions invariant is an elegant architectural constraint. It creates a provable guarantee that /pub redirect is always safe, turning a complex caching correctness problem into a simple boolean check. Most specs bolt on CDN compatibility as an afterthought.

[AI GENERATED - Strength] The type system philosophy (Guarded<T>, Ken<T>, Pii<T>) treats security as a type-level concern rather than a runtime check. This is the same philosophy as Rust's ownership model -- make illegal states unrepresentable. Particularly strong: Ken<T> vs nullable disambiguation prevents a class of access-control bugs where 'absent' and 'unauthorized' are confused.

[AI GENERATED - Strength] The spec explicitly constrains what AI editors may and may not do (e.g., must not introduce Unknown-to-Known merge escalation). This is rare -- most specs don't account for AI as an editor of the spec itself. Forward-thinking for AI-assisted development.

[AI GENERATED - Strength] User/Person separation is not just a naming convention -- it's a capability boundary. Code that has a Person cannot assume that person's privileges. This is a capability-based security pattern that prevents confused deputy attacks at the type level.

[AI GENERATED - Strength] The deliberate exclusion of star ratings for testimonials reflects good product thinking. Aggregated scores are gameable, while curated text testimonials with moderation are harder to manipulate and more useful for developers evaluating libraries.
[AI GENERATED - Gap] Background section item 6 is truncated: 'Once we have can test against storage we can' -- sentence is incomplete. The iterative development roadmap is missing its final step.

[AI GENERATED - Gap] Account recovery flow is explicitly TBD. With OAuth-only auth, if a user loses access to their OAuth provider (e.g., GitHub account compromised), there is no specified way to recover their Vale identity. This is a significant operational gap.

[AI GENERATED - Gap] No rate limiting or abuse prevention specified. The publication flow, account creation, moderation actions, and search all lack rate limiting requirements. The testimonial system could be spammed even with moderation.

[AI GENERATED - Gap] No search specification. The homepage mentions searching for libraries but there is no specification for how search works: full-text, tag-based, backend-filtered, relevance ranking, pagination. This is a core user-facing feature.

[AI GENERATED - Gap] Session management details are sparse. How are session IDs generated? Cookie attributes (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite)? Session expiration/rotation? The spec says 'replace session identifiers' on login but doesn't specify the session security model.

[AI GENERATED - Gap] The orphaned library workflow (when managing group is deleted) is explicitly TBD. For a package repository, abandoned packages are a real operational concern -- dependency resolution, security patches, community takeover.

[AI GENERATED - Gap] No specification for error pages or error handling conventions beyond 'standard 404'. What does 500 look like? How are errors logged? Is there a health check endpoint? What about graceful degradation?
[AI GENERATED - Gap] Locale fallback is mentioned but the full BCP47 fallback algorithm is not specified. Does zh-Hant-TW fall back to zh-Hant, then zh, then en? What about script-only tags?

[AI GENERATED - Gap] The 'Backend' concept (/l/[libraryname]/b/[backendid]) is mentioned in the URL map but never defined as an entity. What is a backend? How does a library get backend-specific docs? What is the relationship between Temper backends and Vale?

[AI GENERATED - Risk] Cyclic group membership is explicitly permitted but the memoized algorithm sets memo[pair]=false before recursion. If group A contains B and B contains A, checking 'is X in A?' would find B, recurse into B, find A (already memo'd as false), and incorrectly report X is not in A. The algorithm may produce false negatives for members that are reachable only through cycles.

[AI GENERATED - Risk] The delta merge rule that null means 'no change' combined with JSON absent-field-equals-null convention means a client cannot intentionally delete a field value. If a user wants to clear their bio, null means 'don't change it'. A separate sentinel value or explicit 'clear' action would be needed but is not specified.

[AI GENERATED - Risk] CSRF via dynamic import() of secrets module: if the secrets endpoint is not properly access-controlled, an attacker embedding an iframe could potentially import the secrets module cross-origin. The spec needs to specify CORS restrictions on the secrets module endpoint.

[AI GENERATED - Risk] The spec allows cryptographic public keys for persons but never specifies their purpose. Are they for package signing? End-to-end encrypted messages? Verified identity? Unsigned public keys attached to a profile have unclear security value.
[AI GENERATED - Risk] LibraryProfile.name is unique and plain text. Name squatting and typosquatting (common in npm/PyPI) are not addressed. No reservation, dispute, or namespace policy is specified.

[AI GENERATED - Risk] The iframe srcdoc approach for Markdown with JavaScript content is mentioned as speculative. Allowing executable content from library READMEs, even sandboxed, introduces significant XSS surface area. The same-origin-disallowed mitigation needs careful specification.

[AI GENERATED - Suggestion] The spec would benefit from a formal entity relationship diagram. The types are well-defined in prose but a visual showing Person -[has]-> PrincipalProfile, Library -[managedBy]-> Group, Testimonial -[about]-> Library, Group -[contains]-> Group (cyclic) would make the relationships immediately graspable. MkDocs Material already has mermaid support configured.

[AI GENERATED - Suggestion] Consider specifying a library deletion/deprecation workflow. Package repositories inevitably need to handle: security advisories, malicious package takedown, author-requested deletion, left-pad scenarios. Even a simple 'yank' (hide from new resolution but allow existing pins) would help.

[AI GENERATED - Suggestion] The privilegedGetPii() pattern could be strengthened with a compile-time lint or annotation that requires a comment explaining why PII is being accessed. This would make the grep audit more useful: instead of just finding access sites, each site would document its justification.

[AI GENERATED - Suggestion] The spec's 'position paper' approach is genuinely novel for software specs. By explaining the WHY behind each architectural choice, it enables AI-assisted implementation where the AI can make locally-correct decisions consistent with the spec's philosophy rather than just pattern-matching against rules.

[AI GENERATED - Suggestion] Consider specifying an audit log requirement. Given the access control complexity (group membership, visibility gates, PII access), an append-only audit log of 'who accessed what when' would be invaluable for compliance and incident response.

[AI GENERATED - Suggestion] The moderation flow is well-specified for UI but lacks webhook/notification hooks. Library managers should be notified when new testimonials enter the queue. Email or in-app notification specification would complete this workflow.

[AI GENERATED - Meta-observation] The spec is simultaneously a product spec, an architecture doc, a security requirements doc, and a teaching document. This multi-purpose nature is its strength (holistic) and weakness (592 lines with no table of contents beyond H2 headers). Splitting into linked docs by concern (security.md, schemas.md, workflows.md) while keeping the position-paper style would improve navigability.

[AI GENERATED - Observation] The spec mentions Temper as the language but uses no Temper syntax for type definitions. All types are described in prose with generic notation (Guarded<T>, List<T>). This is intentional -- the spec precedes implementation -- but creating .temper interface files would formalize the types and enable compiler-checked consistency.

[AI GENERATED - Cross-cutting] The Guarded<T> -> Ken<T> -> /pub equivalence chain: Guarded gates visibility, Ken represents the result of that gating, and the UserContext boolean tracking whether Ken was ever exercised determines /pub redirect. This is a three-layer design where each layer has a distinct responsibility.

[AI GENERATED - Cross-cutting] Group membership is the load-bearing abstraction: it determines Guarded<T> visibility, library access (visibleTo), edit permissions (managedBy roles), testimonial visibility, and profile field access. A bug in group membership propagation would cascade across the entire system.

[AI GENERATED - Cross-cutting] The spec's testing philosophy forms a coherent strategy: abstract DB for test isolation, type-level PII/access markers for static auditing, substring tests for access control, and the memoized membership algorithm designed to be checkable against Cypher for equivalence.
Compare Vale spec with hex.pm
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"first-analysis-with-deciduous","confidence":90,"prompt":"how does it compare to say, hex.pm?"}

nodes.title: [AI GENERATED - Comparison] Both are package repositories but for different ecosystems: hex.pm serves BEAM languages (Erlang/Elixir/Gleam), Vale serves Temper. Hex.pm is mature and running in production (~10K+ packages); Vale is a pre-implementation specification.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Auth: hex.pm uses username/password + API keys + optional TOTP 2FA. Vale specifies OAuth-only (no passwords stored). Vale's approach is simpler but hex.pm's API key system with scoped permissions (read/write per repo) is more flexible for CI/CD automation.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Access control: hex.pm uses package ownership (full/maintainer levels) + organization roles (admin/write/read). Vale uses group-based visibility (managedBy/visibleTo) with nested group membership. Vale's model is more general (groups-of-groups, transitive membership) but more complex to implement.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] CDN strategy: hex.pm separates repo.hex.pm (read-only, Fastly CDN) from hex.pm/api (read/write). Vale uses /pub/[locale] prefix for cacheable public views. Both recognize CDN as critical. Hex.pm's two-endpoint separation is cleaner; Vale's single-URL-with-prefix approach is more complex but serves localized content.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Immutability: hex.pm makes releases immutable after 1 hour (24h for first release). Vale has no immutability policy specified -- a significant gap for a package repository. Immutability is critical for reproducible builds and supply chain security.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Package retirement: hex.pm has 5 retirement reasons (renamed, deprecated, security, invalid, other) where retired packages remain resolvable. Vale has no retirement/deprecation mechanism specified at all. This is a major missing workflow.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Security features: hex.pm has registry signing (RSA/SHA-512), checksum verification, audit logs (public since 2020), vulnerability reporting system, hex diff tool. Vale specifies Pii<T>/Guarded<T> type-level protections and Markdown sanitization but has no package signing, no audit logs, no vulnerability reporting.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Search: hex.pm uses PostgreSQL pg_trgm for fuzzy search with field-specific prefixes (name:, description:, extra:). Vale mentions search on the homepage but has no search specification at all.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Documentation: hex.pm has a dedicated hexdocs.pm service with per-version docs, private docs for orgs, and Typesense-powered search. Vale specifies README-based locale-mapped documentation extraction but no dedicated docs hosting service.
[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Rate limiting: hex.pm has explicit limits (100/min unauthenticated, 500/min authenticated) with standard headers. Vale has no rate limiting specification.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] Moderation: hex.pm has DMCA takedown, dispute resolution policy, code of conduct. Vale has testimonial moderation (queue/accept/reject) which hex.pm doesn't have, but lacks the broader content governance policies.

[AI GENERATED - Comparison] API design: hex.pm cleanly separates read-only repo endpoint (protobuf, gzipped) from read/write API (JSON). Vale uses /json prefix convention on any URL for API access. Hex.pm's protobuf format is more bandwidth-efficient; Vale's JSON-everywhere approach is simpler but heavier.

[AI GENERATED - Vale ahead] Vale's type-level security (Guarded<T>, Ken<T>, Pii<T>) is architecturally more sophisticated than hex.pm's approach. Hex.pm handles access control at the application/API layer; Vale bakes it into the type system so unauthorized data physically cannot escape to rendering code.

[AI GENERATED - Vale ahead] Vale's group-based access model is more expressive than hex.pm's flat org roles. Hex.pm has 3 org roles; Vale has transitive group membership enabling nested orgs, cross-org collaboration, and per-field visibility. This matters for enterprise use cases.

[AI GENERATED - Vale ahead] Vale's locale-aware architecture (BCP47 locale in URLs, locale-mapped docs, localized UI) is far more international than hex.pm which is English-only. For a new package registry, i18n from day one is a strategic advantage.

[AI GENERATED - Vale ahead] Vale's testimonial system (moderated per-library reviews with group-scoped visibility) has no equivalent in hex.pm. Hex.pm has no user-generated content about packages beyond metadata. This could be a differentiator for library discovery.

[AI GENERATED - Hex.pm lessons] Vale should specify release immutability. Hex.pm's 1-hour mutable window then permanent immutability is battle-tested. Without this, Vale is vulnerable to supply chain attacks and broken reproducible builds.

[AI GENERATED - Hex.pm lessons] Vale should specify package retirement with reasons. Hex.pm's retired-but-resolvable model prevents left-pad incidents while still flagging problems. Vale needs this for production viability.

[AI GENERATED - Hex.pm lessons] Vale should specify audit logging. Hex.pm has had audit logs since 2016 (public since 2020). For a system with complex group-based permissions, audit trails are essential for debugging access issues and detecting compromised accounts.

[AI GENERATED - Hex.pm lessons] Vale should specify registry/package signing. Hex.pm signs all registry resources with RSA/SHA-512. Without signing, Vale clients cannot verify that downloaded packages haven't been tampered with by mirrors or CDN compromises.

[AI GENERATED - Hex.pm lessons] Vale should consider separating the read-only package delivery endpoint from the read/write API, as hex.pm does with repo.hex.pm vs hex.pm/api. This is simpler than Vale's /pub prefix approach and easier to put behind CDN.

[AI GENERATED - Hex.pm lessons] Vale's LibraryAutomation secrets are analogous to hex.pm's scoped API keys but less specified. Hex.pm's key permission model (domain/resource/effect) with organization-owned keys for CI/CD is a proven pattern Vale should study.
Derive supply chain security learnings from hex.pm for Vale
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"first-analysis-with-deciduous","confidence":90,"prompt":"lets derive some learnings from Hex for Vale RE supply chain security"}

nodes.title: [AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm CVE-2019-1000012: registry signing was a signing oracle -- signed payloads weren't bound to package name/repo, so a malicious mirror could swap signed registry entries between packages. Fix: add name+repository fields to protobuf and verify on decode. LESSON: Vale must bind any signed artifact to its identity.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm CVE-2026-23940: oversized tarball caused OOM during extraction, killing the server. LESSON: Vale's publication system must enforce size limits on uploaded artifacts before extraction. hex_core defaults: 16MB tarball max, 128MB uncompressed max.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm verification pipeline: 1) fetch gzipped registry, 2) verify RSA-SHA512 signature, 3) verify name+repo binding, 4) download tarball, 5) verify outer SHA-256 checksum against registry, 6) extract. Each step is a gate. Vale needs an equivalent multi-step verification chain.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm immutability: 60-min revert window for releases, 24h for first-ever package release, then permanent. Vale should specify an immutability policy. The window allows fixing 'oops I published my .env' without enabling supply chain manipulation.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm retirement vs deletion: retired packages remain resolvable and fetchable (preserves reproducible builds) but show warnings. 5 reasons: renamed, deprecated, security, invalid, other. Vale MUST have this -- deletion breaks dependency resolution, retirement doesn't.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm audit logs record 30+ action types since 2016 (public since 2020): release.publish, owner.add, key.generate, etc. With Vale's complex group permissions, audit logs are even more critical. Every managedBy/visibleTo change, every publication, every group membership change should be logged.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm typosquatting defense: daily Levenshtein distance check on new package names against existing ones. Vale should implement similar. Additionally, Vale's group-scoped visibility means private library names could leak via typosquat detection -- need to only compare against libraries visible to the registrant.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm has no code execution on install (unlike npm postinstall). Temper compilation is the natural review point. Vale spec should explicitly prohibit install-time code execution in published artifacts. This is a critical supply chain boundary.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm hex diff tool: downloads two versions from registry (not GitHub) and produces a git diff. Ensures comparison reflects what's actually published, not what's in source control. Vale should specify a diff capability since published artifacts may diverge from source repos.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm EEF is now a CVE Numbering Authority. Vale/Temper ecosystem should plan for vulnerability disclosure infrastructure from the start: vulnerability reporting workflow, integration with OSV.dev/GitHub Advisory Database, and a path to CNA status.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Hex.pm scoped API keys: domain/resource/effect model (e.g., api:read, repository:myorg). Vale's LibraryAutomation has opaque write-only secrets. Vale should adopt scoped permissions: publish-only keys, read-only keys, per-library keys, org-level keys. This limits blast radius of key compromise.

[AI GENERATED - Supply Chain] Vale-specific advantage: Vale's Guarded<T>/visibleTo system means private libraries are invisible to unauthorized users. But this creates a unique supply chain risk: if a public library depends on a private library, resolution fails for users outside the group. Vale should specify dependency visibility rules -- public libraries must only depend on public libraries.
Ideate dedicated docs hosting for Vale (like hexdocs.pm)
nodes.metadata_json: {"branch":"first-analysis-with-deciduous","confidence":85,"prompt":"Can we also ideate about a dedicated docs hosting solution baked in with the Vale spec"}

nodes.title: [AI GENERATED - Docs Hosting] Current Vale spec: docs are README*.md files locale-mapped via filename convention, stored in VersionProfile as Map<Locale, Markdown>. Served inline on library page. No dedicated docs subdomain, no per-version hosting, no API docs generation, no search across docs.
ValeDocs as separate subdomain (hexdocs model)
ValeDocs integrated into Vale (docs as first-class routes)
ValeDocs as static site generator with CDN (docs.rs model)

[AI GENERATED - Option Detail] Separate subdomain (docs.vale.dev): dedicated service handles doc rendering. Upload doc tarball on publish -> async processing (like hexdocs via SQS/Broadway). Private docs via auth cookie dance (redirect to Vale for login, encrypted cookie back). Pro: independent scaling, CDN-friendly. Con: auth complexity for private docs, extra service to maintain.

[AI GENERATED - Option Detail] Integrated docs (/l/[name]/docs/[version]/[path]): docs are routes within Vale itself. Leverages existing access control (Guarded<T>, visibleTo). No auth dance needed for private docs. Pro: unified access control, simpler deployment. Con: docs traffic hits the main app, harder to cache (mixed public/private), HTML serving bloats the application.

[AI GENERATED - Option Detail] Static site generation (docs.rs model): on publish, Vale generates static HTML from Temper source + README, stores on S3/GCS. Per-version snapshots. Public docs served entirely from CDN. Private docs require signed URLs or edge auth. Pro: zero runtime cost, massively cacheable. Con: generation pipeline complexity, private doc access is awkward with static files.

[AI GENERATED - Temper Advantage] Temper compiles to multiple backends (JS, Java, Python, etc). ValeDocs could show API docs PER BACKEND -- the same library rendered with the idioms and type signatures of each target language. No other package registry does this. It's a killer feature for a multi-target language.

[AI GENERATED - Temper Advantage] Temper's .temper.md format mixes prose and code. ValeDocs could render these as literate programming docs where the documentation IS the source, with live examples extracted from the actual library code. No separate doc-writing step needed.

[AI GENERATED - Temper Advantage] Since Temper targets JS, ValeDocs could embed live playgrounds (compile Temper to JS, run in browser). The spec already mentions iframe srcdoc experimentation. A sandboxed playground per library would be a major differentiator vs hexdocs/docs.rs/pkg.go.dev.

[AI GENERATED - Docs Hosting] Cross-library search is table stakes. Hexdocs uses Typesense, docs.rs uses a custom engine. Vale's locale-aware architecture means search must be locale-aware too -- searching in Japanese should find Japanese README docs. This is unique to Vale.

[AI GENERATED - Docs Hosting] Vale's /pub equivalence rule applies to docs too. Public library docs should redirect to a CDN-cached /pub version. Private docs (visibleTo != public) cannot. The UserContext boolean tracking already handles this -- docs rendering is just another page that respects the same rule.

[AI GENERATED - Docs Hosting] API doc generation from Temper source: extract type signatures, doc comments, module structure. Store structured doc data (not just rendered HTML) so that: 1) search can index types and functions, 2) cross-library linking works ('this function returns a type from library X'), 3) backend-specific views can be generated.

[AI GENERATED - Docs Hosting] Per-version docs with a version switcher (like docs.rs) is essential. The current spec stores docs in VersionProfile but only serves 'latest' on the library page. Each /l/[name]/v/[semver] should have full docs, and /l/[name] should have the latest non-prerelease docs with a prominent version picker.

[AI GENERATED - Docs Hosting] Private docs access: hexdocs does auth-cookie-dance (redirect to hex.pm, verify, cookie back). Vale can do better -- its integrated group system means the docs service can verify group membership directly via the same UserContext. If docs are a Vale route (not separate subdomain), access control is free.

[AI GENERATED - Docs Hosting Recommendation] Hybrid approach: serve docs as integrated Vale routes (/l/[name]/docs/...) for access control simplicity, but generate static HTML at publish time and serve from CDN for public libraries via /pub. This gets the access control benefit of integration AND the performance benefit of static generation.
Separate subdomain docs hosting (docs.vale approach)
